answers.field_label: Website Address Or Access Description Triennial Results
answers.value:
The most recent triennial wellness assessment results are posted on the Maple Grove Unified School District website at:
https://www.maplegroveusd.org/departments/wellness/triennial-assessment

Printed copies are also available upon request in the District Student Services Office (2145 Oak Ridge Drive, Suite 300, Maple Grove, MN 55369) and in each school’s main office. Families who do not have reliable internet access are informed through a mailed postcard each spring that they can request a printed copy in English, Spanish, or Somali by calling (763) 555-0100 or emailing wellness@maplegroveusd.org. A summary of key findings is included annually in the district’s “Back-to-School Family Guide,” which is sent home with students during the first week of school.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Website Address For Policy Or Access Description
answers.value:
The written Local School Wellness Policy is available online at:
https://www.maplegroveusd.org/board/policies/5500-wellness

The policy can be accessed by navigating from the district homepage (https://www.maplegroveusd.org) to “School Board,” then “Board Policies,” and selecting Policy 5500: Student Wellness. The policy is provided in PDF format and is updated whenever revisions are approved by the School Board. Hard copies are kept in the District Office reception area and in the main office of each school building. Families may request translated copies in Spanish or Somali by contacting the District Translation and Interpretation Services at (763) 555-0164 or translation@maplegroveusd.org.

answers.value:
In addition to the required stakeholder groups, the Maple Grove Unified School District Wellness Committee intentionally includes:

• School Counselors and Social Workers: At least one licensed school counselor and one school social worker participate each year. For the current cycle, this includes Ms. Dana Cho, School Counselor at Maple Grove Middle School, and Mr. Anthony Velasquez, School Social Worker at Lincoln Elementary. They provide insight into the connections between student mental health, attendance, and wellness initiatives such as mindfulness breaks and social-emotional learning.

• Community Health Partners: A representative from North Pines Community Health Clinic, currently Nurse Practitioner Sarah El-Amin, attends quarterly meetings. She shares local health data trends (e.g., childhood obesity rates, asthma prevalence) and helps the committee align school wellness activities with community health resources like free immunization clinics and family nutrition workshops.

• Parks and Recreation Department: The city’s Parks and Recreation Coordinator, Mr. Jason O’Reilly, participates twice a year to coordinate joint-use of facilities and to promote after-school and weekend physical activity programs, such as youth soccer, swimming lessons at the community center, and summer walking clubs.

• Special Education Representatives: A special education teacher or related service provider is invited to ensure that wellness activities are inclusive of students with disabilities. For the current assessment period, Ms. Laura Nguyen, Special Education Teacher at Maple Grove High School, has been a regular member, helping adapt physical activity opportunities and nutrition education for students with mobility or sensory needs.

• Parent-Teacher Organization (PTO) Leaders: While parents at large are represented, the district also invites PTO presidents or wellness liaisons from several schools. For example, Ms. Maria Hernandez, PTO Co-President at Lincoln Elementary, has provided feedback on aligning classroom celebrations with healthier snack guidelines and helped pilot a “walk-to-school” week.

• Student Nutrition Interns: Through a partnership with a nearby community college, one or two dietetic technician or nutrition students participate in a non-voting advisory role each spring semester. They assist with analyzing school menu patterns, conducting student taste-tests for new recipes (e.g., roasted chickpeas, whole-grain flatbread pizzas), and summarizing survey results on student satisfaction with school meals.

These additional stakeholder groups help the committee see wellness not just as a cafeteria or PE issue, but as a whole-child, whole-community effort. Their participation has led to concrete changes, such as revising fundraising guidelines to reduce reliance on candy sales, adding more vegetarian lunch options, and coordinating community 5K events that start and end at school campuses.
answers.reasoning: Pre-filled value from ground truth form

answers.field_label: Notes On Public Involvement Notification And Assessment
answers.value:
The district uses multiple methods to notify the public about wellness policy activities, opportunities for involvement, and the results of the triennial assessment. Each August, before the start of the school year, a detailed “Wellness in Our Schools” section is included in the mailed Back-to-School packet sent to every household with enrolled students. This section highlights:

• A plain-language summary of the wellness policy (including nutrition standards, physical education expectations, and guidelines for fundraisers and classroom celebrations).
• An invitation to join the District Wellness Committee, including typical meeting times (third Wednesday of the month from 4:00–5:30 p.m.), the meeting location (District Office Board Room, 2145 Oak Ridge Drive, Maple Grove, MN 55369), and contact information for the Wellness Coordinator (Miguel A. Ramirez, (763) 555-0198, wellness@maplegroveusd.org).
• A short overview of key data from the most recent triennial assessment, such as the percentage of schools offering daily recess, the proportion of a la carte items that meet Smart Snacks standards, and student survey results on feeling safe and supported at school.

During the school year, the district reinforces this outreach in several ways:

1. School Newsletters and Email Blasts:
   - Each school includes a recurring “Wellness Corner” in monthly newsletters, often featuring practical tips (e.g., packing balanced lunches, incorporating physical activity into family routines) as well as reminders that the full wellness policy and assessment results are available online and in print.
   - The district’s SchoolMessenger system is used twice a year to send a brief email and automated phone message to families, with a direct link to the wellness policy webpage and an invitation to attend upcoming committee meetings.

2. Website and Social Media:
   - The District Wellness page is updated at least annually with meeting dates, agendas, and minutes. The triennial assessment report is prominently linked at the top of the page.
   - When major milestones occur (such as the adoption of updated nutrition standards or the launch of a new after-school physical activity program), these are highlighted on the district homepage and on official social media channels (Facebook and Twitter), along with a reminder that community members are welcome to attend wellness committee meetings.

3. In-Person Outreach and Translation:
   - At fall open houses and curriculum nights, each school sets up a small “Wellness Table” staffed by either the school nurse, PE teacher, or a designated wellness liaison. Flyers in English, Spanish, and Somali explain the wellness policy, list ways families can get involved (e.g., serving on the committee, volunteering for walk-to-school days, supporting healthy classroom parties), and provide contact information.
   - For families with limited English proficiency, interpreters are available at major events, and translated materials are distributed. Teachers and bilingual family liaisons personally encourage parents who have expressed interest in health or nutrition to consider joining the committee or participating in surveys and focus groups.

4. Documentation and Record-Keeping:
   - The Wellness Coordinator maintains a digital folder on the district’s secure server containing: copies of the current and previous versions of the written wellness policy; screenshots and PDFs demonstrating that the policy and triennial assessment have been posted publicly; copies of flyers, email messages, and newsletter excerpts used to invite stakeholders to participate; sign-in sheets and agendas from wellness committee meetings; and any survey instruments and summary reports related to wellness.
   - For example, for the 2023–2026 assessment cycle, the district retained sign-in sheets from all 11 committee meetings, including names and roles (e.g., parent, student, teacher, administrator). One notable detail is that two high school students, Jenna Lee (grade 11) and Marcus Johnson (grade 10), attended nearly every meeting and provided candid feedback on the practicality of proposed changes, such as adjusting lunch schedules to allow enough time to eat and socialize.

5. Reflections and Areas for Improvement:
   - While the district has been consistent in posting documents online and sending general notifications, feedback from parents—especially those with younger children and those working multiple jobs—suggests that many still feel unaware of how to get involved. In response, the committee is piloting more personal, relationship-based outreach, such as having school nurses and social workers mention the wellness committee during individual meetings with families, and asking PTO leaders to include wellness updates as a standing agenda item in their meetings.
   - Another area identified for improvement is making the triennial assessment more accessible and understandable. The current report is fairly long and technical, so for the next cycle, the district plans to create a two-page “family-friendly” summary with infographics (e.g., colorful charts showing increases in daily recess minutes and decreases in sugary drink availability) and concrete examples of changes families might notice, such as healthier vending machine options and new after-school walking clubs.

Overall, public involvement in the wellness policy process has steadily increased. At the first committee meeting in fall 2020, there were 8 attendees; by spring 2023, regular meetings averaged 18–20 participants, including at least 3 parents and 2 students. The district’s long-term goal is to maintain a diverse committee where no single stakeholder group dominates and where feedback from families and students directly shapes policy revisions and implementation priorities.
